request.question: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?

title: The Biggest Loser
Foxburg Country Club
University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band
Transportation in Canada
Human trafficking in Uganda
United 300
300 (film)
North Country Community College
Health in Morocco
Maynard carbine
sentences: The Biggest Loser is a reality television show which started in the United States in 2004.
The show centers on overweight and obese contestants attempting to lose the most weight and to fight for a cash prize.
There are different variations of "The Biggest Loser" around the world.
Each country has made its own adaptation to the show; however, the contestants always have the same goal: to lose the highest percentage of weight (or most weight) to become the Biggest Loser.
There is no minimum or maximum weight limit for the show but most males tend to weigh over or near 300 lb (136 kg).
Females tend to weigh over or near 200 lb (91 kg).
Foxburg Country Club, established in 1887, is the oldest golf course in continuous use in the United States.
It is located in Foxburg, Clarion County, Pennsylvania, United States of America, approximately 55 mi north of Pittsburgh on a hill rising about 300 feet above the Allegheny River.
The course was listed in 2007 as Foxburg Country Club and Golf Course on the National Register of Historic Places.
The clubhouse contains the American Golf Hall of Fame.
The University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band (also known as the Marching Red or The Pride of All Nebraska) is the marching band of the University of Nebraska.
It performs at all home football games.
Founded in 1879, the Marching Red is one of the oldest and best-known collegiate marching bands in the United States.
The band consists of 300 students from over 60 different academic majors from across the campus.
Thanks to the success of the Husker football program, the Marching Red is also one of the most traveled bands in the country having participated in all of the major bowl games.

They have appeared multiple times at the Rose, Fiesta, Orange, Sugar, Cotton, Sun, and Alamo Bowls, and have made single appearances at the Bluebonnet, Liberty, Holiday, Citrus, Independence and Gator Bowls.
On November 19, 2011, the Cornhusker Marching Band performed at Michigan Stadium in front of the largest audience ever to watch the band.

Transportation in Canada, the world's second-largest country in total area, is dedicated to having an efficient, high-capacity multimodal transport spanning often vast distances between natural resource extraction sites, agricultural and urban areas.

Canada's transportation system includes more than 1400000 km of roads, 10 major international airports, 300 smaller airports, 72093 km of functioning railway track, and more than 300 commercial ports and harbours that provide access to the Pacific, Atlantic and Arctic oceans as well as the Great Lakes and the St. Lawrence Seaway.
In 2005, the transportation sector made up 4.2% of Canada's GDP, compared to 3.7% for Canada's mining and oil and gas extraction industries.
Uganda is a source and destination country for men, women, and children trafficked for the purposes of forced labor and sexual exploitation.
Ugandan children are trafficked within the country, as well as to Canada, Egypt, the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia for forced labor and commercial sexual exploitation.
Karamojong women and children are sold in cattle markets or by intermediaries and forced into situations of domestic servitude, sexual exploitation, herding, and begging.

Security companies in Kampala recruit Ugandans to serve as security guards in Iraq where, at times, their travel documents and pay have reportedly been withheld as a means to prevent their departure; these cases may constitute trafficking.
Pakistani, Indian, and Chinese workers are reportedly trafficked to Uganda, and Indian networks traffic Indian children to the country for sexual exploitation.
Children from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (D.R.C.), Rwanda, and Burundi are trafficked to Uganda for agricultural labor and commercial sexual exploitation.

Until August 2006, the terrorist rebel organization, Lord’s Resistance Army (LRA), abducted children and adults in northern Uganda to serve as soldiers, sex slaves, and porters; while no further abductions of Ugandan children have been reported, at least 300 additional people, mostly children, were abducted during the reporting period in the Central African Republic and the D.R.C.
The Government of Uganda does not fully comply with the minimum standards for the elimination of trafficking; however, it is making significant efforts to do so.
United 300 is an American short film that parodies "United 93" (2006) and "300" (2007).
It won the MTV Movie Spoof Award at the MTV Movie Awards 2007.
The short was created by Andy Signore along with some of his friends.
During his speech, he said, roughly, "The film was not making fun of a tragedy; it was a tribute to those who stood up against tyranny".
300 is a 2006 American epic war film based on the 1998 comic series "300" by Frank Miller and Lynn Varley.
Both are fictionalized retellings of the Battle of Thermopylae within the Persian Wars.
The film was directed by Zack Snyder, while Miller served as executive producer and consultant.
It was filmed mostly with a super-imposition chroma key technique, to help replicate the imagery of the original comic book.
North Country Community College is a two-year college of the State University of New York located in Upstate New York.
Founded in 1967, the main campus is in Saranac Lake, New York, and serves Franklin and Essex Counties; enrollment is approximately 1500 students.
It employs 143 permanent and 300 adjunct faculty.
North Country is the only public college located within the Adirondack Park.
The College, located in New York's North Country Region, has additional locations in Malone and Ticonderoga.
In August 2010, North Country Community College was ranked Best Community College in New York and 22nd among the Best Community Colleges in the United States by "Washington Monthly".

The college has also been ranked one of the Top 50 fastest growing community colleges in the United States in "Community College Week Magazine", 101 Best for Vets Colleges in the United States by "Military Times" and a Top 15% Military Friendly Colleges in the U.S., by gijobs.com.
Morocco became an independent country in 1956.
At that time there were only 400 private practitioners and 300 public health physicians in the entire country.
By 1992, the government had thoroughly improved their health care service and quality.
Health care was made available to over 70% of the population.
Programs and courses to teach health and hygiene have been introduced to inform parents and children on how to correctly care for their own and their families' health.
The Maynard carbine was a breech-loaded carbine used by cavalry in the American Civil War.
The First Model was manufactured between 1858 and 1859.
About 5,000 were made.
In United States service it was distributed to the 9th Pennsylvania and 1st Wisconsin cavalry regiments, United States Marines aboard the USS "Saratoga" and the United States Revenue Cutter Service.
About 3,000 Maynard carbines were in Confederate hands during the war; 5,000 in .35 caliber were purchased by Florida, 650 in .50 by Georgia, and 325 in .50 and 300 in .35 by Mississippi.
Around 800 were purchased by militias in South Carolina and Louisiana.
The Second Model or Model 1863 was manufactured between 1863 and 1865.
Over 20,000 were made.
This model lacked the tape primer and stock patchbox, and was used by the 9th and 11th Indiana cavalry regiments and 11th Regiment Tennessee Volunteer Cavalry, among others.
The Maynard had a good reputation for long-range accuracy, and Confederate sharpshooters made extensive use of it, especially during the Siege of Charleston.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?
hop1_docs: Hansen High School | Hansen High School is a high school in Hansen, Idaho.  They were the 2003 state track division 1A champions.They went to 2009 state track in the 300 hurdles, and 800m.  Their rivals are the Murtaugh Red Devils.  Hansen high school has about 300+ students.  It offers athletic programs for both girls and boys such as football, volleyball, basketball, cross country, and track.

Fortschritt ZT 300 | ZT 300 is a series of 20-kN-tractors produced from 1967 to 1984 by the VEB Traktorenwerk Schönebeck.  It succeeded the "RS14 Famulus" series and unlike the Famulus, the ZT 300 series was sold under the brand name Fortschritt.  "ZT 300" stands for the ZT 300 series tractors as well as the initial tractor Fortschritt ZT 300.  In total, 72.382 tractors were made.  The most built model was the ZT 303 introduced in 1972 with automatic all-wheel-drive; in the early 1980s it would cost 81.000 Mark.  Starting in 1983, the ZT 300 series was succeeded by the ZT 320.

Farman F.300 | The Farman F.300 and F.310 were airliners built in France in the early 1930s.  Built to the same general trimotor layout popular with manufacturers of the time, they were high-wing strut braced monoplanes with fixed tailskid undercarriage.  The cockpit and passenger compartment were fully enclosed.  Most went to equip Farman's own airline, whose twelve F.300 variants made up half its fleet in 1931.

KTM 300 | The KTM 300 is a series of 2-stroke enduro off-road motorcycles made by KTM.  They are the Penton-inspired 1984 300 GS, the 300 DMX, the dirt only 300 MXC, European road legal 300 EXC and their current US counterparts the 300 XC (close-ratio) and 300 XC-W (wide-ratio gear box) respectively.  The KTM 300 is designed for difficult off-road conditions.  The 300 EXC and the 300 XC-W have a small headlight, speedometer, tail-light and somewhat softer linkless PDS suspension.  Starting in 2009 all 300's came with electric start.

Northern 300 | The Northern 300 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series stock car race held in 1958, 1959, and from 1967 to 1972 at Trenton Speedway in Trenton, New Jersey.  The debut race in 1958 was 500 laps and 500 mi in length, which was cut down to 150 laps, 150 mi for 1959.  The 1967 and 1968 races were both 300 laps, covering 300 mi .  The track was lengthened from 1 mile to 1.5 miles for 1969, and the final four races were cut down from 300 to 200 laps, while still covering a 300-mile distance.  A race in Trenton was on the 1973 schedule but was rained out on race day and ultimately cancelled.  A 1974 race was on the Winston Cup schedule before the season, but was replaced by the Purolator 500 at Pocono Raceway.  Richard Petty was the only driver to win more than once at Trenton, winning three times.

Mervyn Cawston | Mervyn Cawston (born 4 February 1952) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper.  Active in both England and the United States, Cawston made over 300 career appearances.

Morane-Saulnier MS.300 | The Morane-Saulnier MS.300 and MS.301 were French parasol wing introductory trainer aircraft, first flown in 1930.  They differed only in engine type.  Neither reached production but were developed into two similar trainers, the MS.230 and MS.315, which were made in large numbers.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Hansen High School — [1]: "Hansen High School is a high school in Hansen, Idaho."
- Fortschritt ZT 300 — [2]: "ZT 300 is a series of 20-kN-tractors"
- Farman F.300 — [3]: "The Farman F.300 and F.310 were airliners built in France"
- KTM 300 — [4]: "The KTM 300 is a series of 2-stroke enduro off-road motorcycles made by KTM."
- Northern 300 — [5]: "The Northern 300 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series stock car race"
- Mervyn Cawston — [6]: "Cawston made over 300 career appearances."
- Morane-Saulnier MS.300 — [7]: "The Morane-Saulnier MS.300 and MS.301 were French parasol wing introductory trainer aircraft"

Ambiguities:
- "300" matches multiple distinct items: "ZT 300", "Farman F.300", "KTM 300", "Northern 300", "MS.300".
- "United 300" does not appear in the passages (no matching excerpt or page present).

Bridge / missing fact:
- The country where "United 300" was made (country-of-origin/manufacture) is not present.
- Which specific "300" the question means (which of the multiple "300" items) is unresolved; for some "300" items country is given (Farman F.300, MS.300 → France) but it's unclear if those are the intended "300".
- Need explicit confirmation that both "United 300" and the intended "300" share the same country.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch the page "United 300" (or run search query: "United 300 site:en.wikipedia.org") to locate the target and its country-of-origin.
- Fetch "300 (disambiguation)" or run: "300 (disambiguation) site:en.wikipedia.org" to identify candidate "300" pages.
- If the intended "300" is the film, fetch "300 (film)" (page) to get country; if it's another item, fetch its specific page (e.g., "Farman F.300", "KTM 300", "Fortschritt ZT 300", "Morane-Saulnier MS.300").
- Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: No. 300 Polish Bomber Squadron | No. 300 (Polish) "Land of Masovia" Bomber Squadron (Polish: "300 Dywizjon Bombowy "Ziemi Mazowieckiej"" ) was a Polish World War II bomber unit.  It was fighting alongside the Royal Air Force and operated from airfields in the United Kingdom.

LR-300 | The LR 300 is an American rifle and conversion kit designed by gunsmith Allan Zitta and manufactured by Z-M Weapons.  The "LR" stands for "Light Rifle" and 300 is for 300 meters, the effective range of the rifle with a standard 55-grain FMJ bullet.  It is based on the AR-15 and is broadly similar to the United States service rifle, the M16.
Alsco 300 | The Alsco 300 is a NASCAR Xfinity Series race held at Kentucky Speedway in Sparta, Kentucky, United States.  The distance of the race is 300 miles (482.803 km).

.300 Norma Magnum | The .300 Norma Magnum is a cartridge that has begun to gain popularity in the long range shooting community.  The .300 Norma Magnum is a .338 Norma Magnum case necked down to .308 caliber.  This cartridge was named .300 Norma Magnum to differentiate it from the .308 Norma Magnum designed in 1960.  The United States Special Operations Command (SOCOM) has selected .300 Norma Magnum as their new Advanced Sniper Rifle Cartridge (2016).
Community Unit School District 300 (disambiguation) | Community Unit School District 300 (disambiguation)

KTM 300 | The KTM 300 is a series of 2-stroke enduro off-road motorcycles made by KTM.  They are the Penton-inspired 1984 300 GS, the 300 DMX, the dirt only 300 MXC, European road legal 300 EXC and their current US counterparts the 300 XC (close-ratio) and 300 XC-W (wide-ratio gear box) respectively.  The KTM 300 is designed for difficult off-road conditions.  The 300 EXC and the 300 XC-W have a small headlight, speedometer, tail-light and somewhat softer linkless PDS suspension.  Starting in 2009 all 300's came with electric start.

The short was created by Andy Signore along with some of his friends.  During his speech, he said, roughly, "The film was not making fun of a tragedy; it was a tribute to those who stood up against tyranny".
response.summary_2: United 300 is an American short film; the provided passages do not state the country where 300 was made.
